final_response:
Bucharest is the capital and largest city of Romania, known in various languages as 'Haaptstad vu Rumänien,' 'Hauptstadt Rumäniens,' 'Kryeqyteti i Rumanisë,' and 'thủ đô và là thành phố lớn nhất của Romania.' The city is located in Romania and carries the motto 'The Homeland and my right.' It is classified as part of the București-Ilfov region.

Covering a total area of 240.0 square kilometers, Bucharest's metropolitan area extends to 1,803.0 square kilometers. The city sits at an elevation of 55.8 meters and is part of the Development Regions of Romania. It is also associated with Ilfov County and the historical region of Muntenia. Lake Dâmbovița is the nearest significant body of water to the city.

Bucharest has a population of 1,716,961, making it the most populous city in Romania. The metropolitan area is home to 2,303,505 people. The city has a population density of 7,196 inhabitants per square kilometer, while the metropolitan area has a density of 1,277 inhabitants per square kilometer. Bucharest is home to diverse communities, including Turks, Germans, and Romani people of Romania.

The city operates under a mayor–council form of government. Its area code is +40 31, and postal codes range from 0100xx-0201xx, 0201xx-0300xx, and 0365xx. Bucharest observes Eastern European Time and Eastern European Summer Time, with UTC offsets of +02:00 and +03:00, respectively.

Bucharest was founded by Vlad the Impaler and has played a significant role in Romanian history. It was the site of the Wallachian Revolution of 1848, the Raids on Bucharest, the Battle of Bucharest, and the Bombing of Bucharest during World War II. The city has served as the capital of the Socialist Republic of Romania, Wallachia, and the Kingdom of Romania, and was the largest city during the Kingdom of Romania under Fascism.

Numerous important institutions and companies are based in Bucharest, including BRD – Groupe Société Générale, Căile Ferate Române, Bucharest Tower Center, and the Military Technical Academy. The University of Agronomic Sciences and Veterinary Medicine of Bucharest is also located here. Major transportation routes such as DN6, the A1, A2, and A3 motorways, and the Bucharest–Giurgiu Motorway all start in the city. The Basarab Overpass, Romaero, Fun Labs, Mega Image, First Bank, and Societatea Pentru Exploatări Tehnice are among the many businesses and facilities in Bucharest. The city is also a manufacturing and assembly site for vehicles like the A Vlaicu II, Lada Kalina (first generation), Lada Priora, and Lada Niva. Bucharest is encircled by the Centura București and the Bucharest Ring Motorway. The Constanța railway station serves the city, and CFRNA targets its airport. Bucharest owns the Stadionul Național (1953) and the FCSB Arena Națională.

Several political and governmental organizations have their headquarters in Bucharest, including the Social Liberal Union, the Social Liberal Humanist Party, Dan Air, and the Ministry of Internal Affairs of Romania. The Foreign Ministry of the Independent State of Croatia also has a child organization in the city.

Bucharest is an important religious center, serving as the diocese for figures such as Patriarch Nicodim of Romania, Mihai Frățilă, Patriarch Daniel of Romania, Teoctist Arăpașu, and Patriarch Iustin of Romania. The Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Bucharest is based here. The city is the place of canonization for Dionysius Exiguus, Constantin Brâncoveanu and his sons, Stephen the Great, and Neagoe Basarab. Major shrines in Bucharest are dedicated to Constantin Brâncoveanu and Vladimir Ghika, who was also beatified in the city.

Many notable individuals have studied in Bucharest, including Zoe Țapu, Florin-Alexandru Alexe, and Laurențiu Rebega. Alex Gâlmeanu, Alina Eremia, and Daniel Ioniță received their education in the city, while Constantin Herold attended college here.

Bucharest is a key military center, hosting the garrisons of the Romanian Naval Forces, Romanian Military Police, Romanian Land Forces, and the Royal Romanian Air Force.

The city is a major hub for media and culture. Radio România Muzical and Virgin Radio Romania broadcast in Bucharest, and Dance FM and the Bucharest Bar serve the region. Songs such as 'Tornerò' by Mihai Trăistariu, 'Vanilla Chocolat,' and 'Cherry Pop' were recorded in the city. Bucharest is also known for the Joffre cake and is home to personalities like Silviu Prigoană. Musical groups such as Mandinga, Kamara Ghedi, ConTempo Quartet, and Elena Gheorghe hail from Bucharest. The city is associated with sports clubs and events, including Gheorghiță Ștefan, Daniela Druncea, CS Smart Sport Bucharest, Faur București, ASC Daco-Getica București, FC Carmen București, FC Venus București, and the BRD Năstase Țiriac Trophy tournaments of 2011 and 2012.

Bucharest is the birthplace of figures such as Cristian Nemescu, Arthur Caesar, George Ogăraru, and Lazăr Edeleanu. It is also the place of death for Cristian Nemescu, Sergiu Nicolaescu, Lazăr Edeleanu, and Nicolae Golescu. Residents include Petre Cișmigu, Alexandra Cadanțu-Ignatik, and Irina-Camelia Begu. The city is the resting place of Nicolae Tonitza, Augustin Buzura, Alecu Filipescu-Vulpea, and Petre Ispirescu. Institutions in Bucharest have been associated with Erwin Friedlander, Irinel Popescu, and Ștefan S. Nicolau, while Mircea Dumitrescu has been an employer in the city. Jacques Hérold, Roberto Dutesco, and Marilena Murariu received training in Bucharest.

Architects such as Carol Benesch, Haralamb H. Georgescu, and Toma N. Socolescu have contributed significant buildings to the city's landscape.

Bucharest has also served as the constituency district for politicians like Crin Antonescu and Nicolae Fleva during their respective tenures.

history_of_steps.agent_output:
Bucharest is the capital and largest city of Romania, known in various languages as 'Haaptstad vu Rumänien,' 'Hauptstadt Rumäniens,' 'Kryeqyteti i Rumanisë,' and 'thủ đô và là thành phố lớn nhất của Romania.' The city is located in Romania and carries the motto 'The Homeland and my right.' It is classified as part of the București-Ilfov region.

Covering a total area of 240.0 square kilometers, Bucharest's metropolitan area extends to 1,803.0 square kilometers. The city sits at an elevation of 55.8 meters and is part of the Development Regions of Romania. It is also associated with Ilfov County and the historical region of Muntenia. Lake Dâmbovița is the nearest significant body of water to the city.

Bucharest has a population of 1,716,961, making it the most populous city in Romania. The metropolitan area is home to 2,303,505 people. The city has a population density of 7,196 inhabitants per square kilometer, while the metropolitan area has a density of 1,277 inhabitants per square kilometer. Bucharest is home to diverse communities, including Turks, Germans, and Romani people of Romania.

The city operates under a mayor–council form of government. Its area code is +40 31, and postal codes range from 0100xx-0201xx, 0201xx-0300xx, and 0365xx. Bucharest observes Eastern European Time and Eastern European Summer Time, with UTC offsets of +02:00 and +03:00, respectively.

Bucharest was founded by Vlad the Impaler and has played a significant role in Romanian history. It was the site of the Wallachian Revolution of 1848, the Raids on Bucharest, the Battle of Bucharest, and the Bombing of Bucharest during World War II. The city has served as the capital of the Socialist Republic of Romania, Wallachia, and the Kingdom of Romania, and was the largest city during the Kingdom of Romania under Fascism.

Numerous important institutions and companies are based in Bucharest, including BRD – Groupe Société Générale, Căile Ferate Române, Bucharest Tower Center, and the Military Technical Academy. The University of Agronomic Sciences and Veterinary Medicine of Bucharest is also located here. Major transportation routes such as DN6, the A1, A2, and A3 motorways, and the Bucharest–Giurgiu Motorway all start in the city. The Basarab Overpass, Romaero, Fun Labs, Mega Image, First Bank, and Societatea Pentru Exploatări Tehnice are among the many businesses and facilities in Bucharest. The city is also a manufacturing and assembly site for vehicles like the A Vlaicu II, Lada Kalina (first generation), Lada Priora, and Lada Niva. Bucharest is encircled by the Centura București and the Bucharest Ring Motorway. The Constanța railway station serves the city, and CFRNA targets its airport. Bucharest owns the Stadionul Național (1953) and the FCSB Arena Națională.

Several political and governmental organizations have their headquarters in Bucharest, including the Social Liberal Union, the Social Liberal Humanist Party, Dan Air, and the Ministry of Internal Affairs of Romania. The Foreign Ministry of the Independent State of Croatia also has a child organization in the city.

Bucharest is an important religious center, serving as the diocese for figures such as Patriarch Nicodim of Romania, Mihai Frățilă, Patriarch Daniel of Romania, Teoctist Arăpașu, and Patriarch Iustin of Romania. The Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Bucharest is based here. The city is the place of canonization for Dionysius Exiguus, Constantin Brâncoveanu and his sons, Stephen the Great, and Neagoe Basarab. Major shrines in Bucharest are dedicated to Constantin Brâncoveanu and Vladimir Ghika, who was also beatified in the city.
